title: Italy Strikes Hit easyJet and Airports on July 21
excerpt: A nationwide easyJet crew strike and ground-handling walkouts hit Italian airports on July 21, 2026. Here's what to do if you're flying that day.
content:
If you have a flight touching Italy on Tuesday, July 21, 2026, put it on your radar now. A cluster of aviation strikes is set to hit airports from Milan to Naples to Sardinia that day, the latest in what has already been an unusually disruptive month for Italian air travel following a nationwide walkout on July 5 that produced roughly 417 delays and 21 cancellations, concentrated at Rome Fiumicino and Milan Malpensa.

The biggest single piece of the July 21 action is a 24-hour strike by easyJet's Italian cabin crew, called by the USB Lavoro Privato union and running midnight to midnight across the airline's entire Italian network - so this isn't limited to one base or route, it covers every easyJet flight touching Italy that day. Crew for the Lithuanian carrier UAB DAT LT are also striking for the full 24 hours.

On top of that, ground-handling staff are walking out at several airports simultaneously. At Milan Malpensa, workers for handling agents ALHA and MLE-BCUBE are striking for 24 hours, and FedEx's cargo ground operations there are affected too - Malpensa is coming off the July 5 strike as one of the two hardest-hit airports in Italy this month, so a second round of handling disruption in the same three weeks is a real risk for anyone connecting through it. Lamezia Terme in Calabria has a 24-hour walkout by Sacal Ground Handling staff. Naples and Salerno airports see a shorter, four-hour work-to-rule from 10am to 2pm involving GH Napoli, Sky Service and Bourelly Health Service employees. And in Sardinia, ground staff at both Olbia (Geasar) and Alghero (Sogeaal) airports are striking from 1pm to 5pm.

As with prior Italian aviation strikes this year, national rules require airlines to guarantee a core schedule during two protected bands: 7am-10am and 6pm-9pm. Flights scheduled inside those windows are supposed to operate close to normal regardless of the walkout; everything outside them, especially midday and early-afternoon departures, is where cancellations and long ground-handling queues are most likely to land.

This wave doesn't end on the 21st, either. Italian outlets are also flagging additional rail and local-transport strikes for July 23 and 24, so if your Italy trip involves a train connection to or from the airport around that same week, it's worth checking that separately closer to the date rather than assuming only the 21st is at risk.

So what should you actually do? If you're booked on easyJet to, from, or within Italy on July 21, check your flight status directly on easyJet's site or app in the 24-48 hours before departure - a nationwide 24-hour crew strike on a single carrier is exactly the kind of action that produces late cancellations and rebookings, and easyJet has typically messaged affected passengers directly once its operational plan for a strike day is finalized. If you're connecting through Malpensa, Naples, Salerno, Olbia, Alghero or Lamezia Terme that day on any airline, build in extra time for baggage and turnaround, since ground-handling strikes slow down every flight passing through the affected handling companies, not just one carrier. Where you have flexibility, favor a flight departing in the 7-10am or 6-9pm protected bands over a midday one. And know your rights under EU Regulation 261/2004: airlines still owe you a choice of rerouting, refund or care (meals, hotel if needed) when a strike disrupts your flight, even though cash compensation is often not owed for strike-related cancellations since regulators generally treat them as extraordinary circumstances - don't let an airline tell you rerouting or a refund isn't available, because that part of the rule still applies.

Italy's summer has already shown that these strikes tend to come in waves rather than one-off events, so if you're booking new Italian travel for later in the summer, it's worth a quick check of Italy's national transport strike calendar before you lock in a tight connection, particularly if Malpensa is part of your routing.
